Evaluation: The current communication protocol effectively improves prediction accuracy of critical state dimensions across all agents compared to no communication. Health-related dimensions and last action information are well conveyed, leading to near-perfect recognition in those areas. However, dimensions related to absolute coordinates (X and Y) of agents and the Ultralisk show uneven improvement and relatively lower success rates, especially for some agents (notably agents 2, 3, and 4). This indicates that while the shared relative enemy position and personal health/actions help, the protocol lacks sufficient cues for consistent absolute spatial awareness and synchronization among agents. Moreover, the protocol does not explicitly represent which agents have reliable observations (e.g., enemy visibility), leading to asymmetric inference capabilities that are not directly addressed. Consequently, some agents struggle to accurately infer absolute positioning and enemy state when they do not have direct observations or when teammates’ messages do not clarify their observation confidence or behavioral context fully. Overall, the protocol supports coordination but falls short in enabling all agents to synchronize their understanding of spatial and situational states robustly across partial observability and diverse observation availability.
Missing_Information_Hypothesis: The current messages omit explicit indicators of observation reliability per sender (e.g., whether the enemy or specific allies are visible to the sender), which is critical for recipients to weigh the trustworthiness and relevance of shared information. Without this, agents cannot differentiate between reliable and guess-based information from peers, leading to inconsistent inferences. Additionally, the protocol does not include self-behavioral constraints such as movement possibilities or recent movement intentions that could help peers infer positional changes or predict state transitions. Since absolute coordinates are not directly observable and must be inferred, the lack of shared behavioral and observation confidence cues prevents agents from effectively aligning their spatial and tactical models. Therefore, the missing key information is explicit observation confidence flags and self-behavioral indicators that can support disambiguation and synchronization of state inference across agents.
Improvement_Suggestions: Incorporate explicit visibility flags in each message indicating whether the sender currently sees the Ultralisk and each visible ally (e.g., a small binary vector for enemy visible and ally visible flags). This will allow receivers to condition their trust and fusion of information based on sender observation reliability.

Add movement possibility indicators (the current ability to move north, south, east, west) from the sender’s own observation into the message. These behavioral cues help peers infer positional constraints and potential movement, aiding absolute coordinate inference.

Include a summarized recent action or intention embedding beyond last action, such as a short history or an aggregated movement tendency, to improve prediction of state transitions and agent positioning.

Instead of only broadcasting relative enemy position if visible, also include a confidence score or a visibility mask per message element to explicitly mark which parts of the message are reliable versus inferred or zeroed.
Add a compact representation of observed ally states (e.g., health and visibility flags) to help agents understand the team’s global status and positional relations better.
Maintain the sender identity one-hot encoding to preserve message traceability but avoid custom peer-specific messages; keep the message broadly useful for all agents.

By augmenting messages with these observation confidence and behavioral cues, agents will better align their internal state representations, reducing asymmetric knowledge gaps and uneven prediction performance especially for spatial and enemy-related dimensions.
